title: North Korea as It Is: Rethinking U.S. Nuclear Strategy
content:
It was never North Korea’s intention to get rid of its nuclear capabilities, meaning the regime was always ready to expand its nuclear arsenal exponentially. Kim Jong Un affirmed this direction in a speech to North Korea’s parliament, declaring that the government will continue to consolidate its irreversible status as a nuclear power. To effectively deal with North Korea, the United States must scrap its single-minded focus on denuclearization and its overreliance on sanctions. Although many policymakers have implicitly accepted this reality, few will propose it publicly because insiders see it as equivalent to surrender. Denuclearization remains a noble goal, but past policy failures and Pyongyang's dogged determination have made it unattainable for now.

Washington needs to shift the logic of its strategy from disarming North Korea’s nukes to achieving immediate goals that will make the United States more secure. Protecting the U.S. homeland has become the most pressing priority as North Korea’s capabilities have evolved. Over the last 30 years, North Korea’s ability to target the United States has evolved from a remote possibility to a very real danger. The range of some North Korean intercontinental ballistic missiles now extends to the continental United States. Furthermore, Pyongyang already possesses enough mobile launchers and missiles to potentially overwhelm U.S. missile defenses.

Even the slightest sign of U.S. military action could trigger a dangerous escalation, and there is no guarantee that the threat of retaliation would deter Kim from acting. Critics frequently call for ramping up economic and financial sanctions, hoping to create enough chaos to accelerate the regime’s collapse. However, the effectiveness of sanctions has drastically decreased because countries like China and Russia are now undercutting the UN sanctions regime. Chinese bilateral trade with North Korea has reached historic highs, and commercial satellite imagery shows border crossings teeming with new trade. Ultimately, what the United States needs is a pragmatic interim solution to protect homeland security and establish a stable, cold peace.
